ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Lemon Rice. Nearest parent is a tempered rice dish. A new concept covering South Indian variety rice dishes is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tempered/seasoned rice dish, South Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SL used as closest parent for South Indian tempered rice; no exact code exists for lemon rice specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Lemon Rice as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of lemon rice is approximately 250 g, typical of a South Indian restaurant serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05100073; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under Food Code F05100073, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian food composition data for South Indian tempered rice dishes. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Analogous Tempered Rice Preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; estimated for lemon rice based on cooked white rice GI with adjustment for fat tempering and lemon juice acidification
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 estimated from cooked white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database); downward adjustment applied for fat tempering (oil, peanuts) and lemon juice acidification, which are known to reduce glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (66 × 32.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 53.6. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 495 mg per 250 g plate; primarily from added salt during preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–180 g and request reduced salt during preparation; lemon juice contributes potassium (180 mg per 250 g serving) which may partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~66) and high glycaemic load (~54) per standard 250 g plate based on white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit lemon rice to 150 g per serving, pair with a protein-rich side such as dal or low-fat yogurt to blunt the glycaemic response, and note that lemon juice vitamin C may improve non-haem iron absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, and cholesterol-free; peanuts contribute unsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemon rice is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it a heart-friendly grain dish when consumed in moderate portions; the peanut content provides unsaturated fatty acids and the turmeric tempering contributes curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) and lemon juice (vitamin C) in the tempering
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric in the tempering provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties; lemon juice provides vitamin C which supports collagen synthesis and enhances non-haem iron absorption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon Rice is a classic South Indian variety rice dish — cooked white rice tossed with a tangy, citrus-bright tempering of oil, mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, curry leaves, dried red chilli, turmeric, and fresh lemon juice, garnished with roasted peanuts. The turmeric turns the rice a vibrant yellow, and the lemon provides a sharp, refreshing acidity balanced by the nuttiness of peanuts and the aromatic pop of mustard seeds. Often served at room temperature, lemon rice is a popular packed lunch, temple prasad offering, and festival food across Tamil Nadu and Karnataka. In Singapore, it is served at South Indian and mamak restaurants as a specialty rice dish. Nutritionally, a 250 g plate provides approximately 470 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 13 g fat, and 81 g carbohydrate. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It carries a medium glycaemic index (~66) and a high glycaemic load per full plate (~54), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium per plate is approximately 495 mg — moderate for hypertension management. The dish is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it suitable for cardiovascular health when consumed in appropriate portions.
common_user_queries: lemon rice vs plain rice nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05100073), an approved primary source for Singapore dishes, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (cooked white rice) with documented adjustment rationale. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this South Indian tempered rice preparation is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo Database or ICMR-NIN as of the access date; only total fat and saturated fat are available. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo Database entry for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a fully plant-based dish. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D-containing ingredients present. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted downward for fat tempering and lemon juice acidification effects on glycaemic response; confidence 0.9. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (66 × 32.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 53.6. Hindi local name 'नींबू चावल' (Nimbu Chawal) added to satisfy mandatory Singaporean dish multilingual requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). FoodEx2 code A03SL used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for lemon rice specifically.
